mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-AMG C63 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M156 engine typically costs £5,000–£8,500 supply only; a reconditioned M177 costs £6,000–£11,000; a reconditioned M139l hybrid costs £10,000–£15,000. Used units are available from £2,000–£5,000. Fitting adds £2,000–£3,500. A replacement M156 engine can cost up to £11,744. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your C63.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-AMG C63 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends entirely on which generation. The M156 (W204) has well-documented weaknesses — head bolt failure (pre-2011) and camshaft/lifter wear. The M177 (W205) suffers from ignition coil failures and cooling system issues. The M139l (W206) is complex and unproven long-term. With diligent maintenance, all three can be reliable, but the M156 demands the most preventative attention.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-AMG C63?

acceptedAnswer.text: M156: head bolt failure (pre-2011 — the most critical concern), camshaft and lifter wear, carbon build-up on intake valves, crankcase breather valve failure. M177: ignition coil failure (recall issued), high oil and water temperatures, cooling system issues. M139l: hybrid system complexity, lack of V8 character.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-AMG C63 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: C63 values remain strong — a well-kept W204 is often worth £15,000–£30,000, W205 cars fetch £25,000–£45,000, and W206 cars are £60,000+. A £5,000–£15,000 reconditioned engine is 15–40% of the vehicle's value — apply the Mercedes 70% scrap threshold. If the car is a cherished example, replacement is often worthwhile.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-AMG C63 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. W204 (2007–2014) uses M156; W205 (2014–2021) uses M177; W206 (2021+) uses M139l. For W204, check the engine number — #60658+ has revised head bolts. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M156 head bolts, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The head bolts can be replaced — they are a known weak point. Replacement costs £5,000–£6,000. If the bolts fail and cause head gasket problems, the engine can be destroyed. A preventative head bolt replacement (with aftermarket studs) is far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£5,000+). At the first sign of clicking on cold start or coolant loss on a pre-2011 M156, book an inspection immediately.

hero.h1: Mercedes-AMG C63 Engine Replacement — Compare Prices & Get Quotes
hero.subHeadline: The C63 AMG has worn three legendary powertrains over its lifetime — the thundering 6.2-litre M156 V8, the twin-turbo 4.0-litre M177 V8, and the hybridised 2.0-litre M139l four-cylinder. Each has its own character, reliability profile and running costs. Get quotes from UK specialists for reconditioned, rebuilt and used units today.

eraMap.title: The C63 THROUGH TIME — Era Map

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current C63 AMG market. The M156 is the cheapest to replace; the M177 commands a premium; the M139l hybrid is the most expensive due to its complexity. A replacement M156 engine can cost up to £11,744. Head bolt replacement on the M156 costs approximately £5,000–£6,000.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a C63 AMG engine typically takes 14–20 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The M177's hot-vee design and the M139l's hybrid system add significant complexity.

problems.title: M156 Head Bolt Failure (W204 — Pre-2011)
problems.symptoms: Clicking noises on cold start, rising oil level, coolant loss requiring frequent top-ups, head gasket failure, bent rods, eventual engine failure
problems.replacementCost: £5,000–£8,500 (if head bolt failure destroys the engine)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: this is the single biggest risk on the early M156

problems.recommendation: The M156's head bolts corrode and fail due to a design flaw. The bolts stretch during installation, exposing the tops to corrosive elements. The issue affects C63s up to the 2011 model year (engine #60658 onwards received revised bolts). If you're buying a pre-2011 C63, check the engine number. If it's below #60658, budget for head bolt replacement immediately. Many owners recommend using aftermarket studs instead of bolts. A £5,000–£6,000 repair is far cheaper than a £5,000–£8,500 engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full head bolt failure guide

problems.title: M156 Camshaft and Lifter Wear (W204)
problems.symptoms: Ticking or tapping noise from the top of the engine on cold start, reduced performance, metallic debris in oil, eventual camshaft failure

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear ticking from the top of the engine, have it inspected immediately

problems.recommendation: The M156 suffers from premature camshaft and lifter wear. A class action lawsuit was filed in 2011 alleging that Mercedes knew about the problem. The issue is caused by soft metal in the camshafts that was improperly heat-treated. If you notice ticking or tapping on cold start, have the camshafts and lifters inspected immediately. Using the correct MB-spec oil and changing it regularly can help prevent this issue. If caught early, lifter replacement is far cheaper than a full camshaft replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full camshaft and lifter wear guide

problems.title: M177 Ignition Coil Failure (W205)
problems.symptoms: Misfire under load, rough idle, engine warning light, reduced power, CEL illumination
problems.recommendation: MB issued a product recall in 2018 for faulty ignition coils on M177 engines (part A1779069500). If your car hasn't had the recall completed, check with your dealer. Replacing a faulty coil with the same part number is not recommended — you'd be replacing a faulty component with another of the same design. If you experience misfires or a CEL, have the ignition system inspected. A £300–£600 coil replacement is far cheaper than the consequences of a misfire damaging the catalytic converters.

failureLink.label: Read the full ignition coil failure guide

problems.title: M177 Cooling System and Oil Temperature Issues (W205)
problems.symptoms: High oil and water temperatures, performance loss, overheating warnings, reduced power under sustained load
problems.recommendation: The M177's hot-vee design generates extreme heat loads. The engine tends to have high oil and water temperatures even in standard production form. Insufficient cooling can lead to overheating and performance loss. If you track your C63 or drive it hard, consider upgrading the cooling system. Check coolant and oil temperatures regularly. If you notice temperatures climbing, have the cooling system inspected. A £500–£2,000 cooling system upgrade is far cheaper than a £6,000–£10,000 engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full cooling system failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: C63 AMG — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Essential upgrade — far cheaper than a full engine replacement
Catch early; cheaper than a full engine
Walnut blasting — well-understood by AMG specialists
Upgrade cooling — essential for track use
Labour-intensive; catch before debris damages the engine

buyingChecks.title: Buying This C63 AMG? Check These First
buyIf: * W204 (2011+) with revised head bolts (engine #60658+) — the defining weakness addressed
* W205 with documented ignition coil recall work completed — a common failure point
* Full service history with correct MB-spec oil — all three engines are unforgiving of neglect
* Evidence of carbon cleaning on M156 — intake valves benefit from walnut blasting
* W206 with comprehensive warranty — the hybrid system is complex and expensive to repair
avoidIf: * W204 (pre-2011) with no proof of head bolt work — you're buying a known risk
* Any ticking from the top of the engine on M156 — camshaft/lifter wear is likely
* W205 with misfire codes and no ignition coil history — coil failures are common
* W206 with any hybrid system warnings — repairs are expensive
* Poor service history — all three engines are unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The C63 AMG is a genuine performance icon across all three generations. The W204 M156 is a naturally aspirated masterpiece with well-documented weaknesses — head bolt failure (pre-2011) and camshaft/lifter wear. The W205 M177 is a twin-turbo V8 with a hot-vee design, suffering from ignition coil failures and cooling system issues. The W206 M139l is a hybridised four-cylinder that delivers astonishing power but lacks the character of its V8 predecessors. Buy on history, maintain on schedule, and any C63 will reward you with one of the most engaging driving experiences available.

cta.label: → Compare C63 AMG engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-AMG C63 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start (M156) — Listen for ticking from the top of the engine (camshaft/lifter warning) and clicking (head bolt warning on pre-2011 cars)
2. Service History — including which generation (W204, W205 or W206) and evidence of major maintenance
3. Engine Number (W204) — Check if it's above #60658 (revised head bolts)
4. Coolant Level — Any unexplained loss? (head bolt or cooling system warning)
5. Oil Level — Is the oil level rising? (coolant mixing with oil — head bolt failure warning)
6. Ignition Coil History (W205) — Has the recall been completed?
7. Misfire Codes (W205) — Any stored codes? (ignition coil or injector issues)
8. Hybrid System Health (W206) — Any warnings or reduced power? (complex system — ensure warranty)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows match Section 1B's Era Map exactly — three engine families across the C63's history.
